pa_request.expected_outcome: Improved renal function with ability to discontinue chronic dialysis and improved functional status while maintaining careful perioperative cardiovascular management.

225.bio_narrative: Meredith Grey is a 45-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas, who has remained actively engaged in her health care while balancing a demanding professional life and family responsibilities. Over the past several years she has managed chronic cardiovascular conditions including atherosclerotic heart disease (I25.10), essential hypertension (I10), and hyperlipidemia (E78.5). She has been consistent with prescribed risk-reduction therapy including metoprolol, losartan, and atorvastatin, and previously completed a course of cardiac rehabilitation to support endurance and conditioning. In late 2025 and early 2026 she began to notice worsening fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, and generalized weakness that were no longer explained by work stress alone. At the 01-20-2026 specialist visit she reported persistent low energy, difficulty completing routine daily activities, and prolonged recovery after exertion. Subsequent evaluation demonstrated progression to chronic kidney disease stage 5 (N18.5) and end stage renal disease (N18.6) requiring ongoing in-center hemodialysis (Z99.2). encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: Patient presents for routine follow-up and medication review for chronic conditions including atherosclerotic heart disease, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. She reports good adherence to Lopressor, Cozaar, and Lipitor with no side effects. Denies chest pain, dyspnea on exertion, syncope, or palpitations.

Review of Systems: Negative for fever, cough, chest pain, edema, dizziness, or new exercise intolerance. Reports regular physical activity and stable appetite.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing female in no acute distress. Cardiovascular exam: regular rate/rhythm, no murmurs, no peripheral edema. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen soft, non-tender. Neurologic exam grossly intact.

Assessment: I25.10 stable on beta-blocker; I10 controlled; E78.5 on statin therapy.

Plan: Continue metoprolol, losartan, atorvastatin. Reinforce heart-healthy diet and activity. Routine labs to be monitored through primary care. Return in 6 months or sooner for symptoms.

Plan: Order labs including CBC and iron studies; refer to hematology for anemia evaluation. Begin/continue renal-supportive medications through dialysis unit. Discuss transplant evaluation pathway and initiate preliminary workup.

images.findings: Non-contrast CT obtained for preoperative planning prior to requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy. Native kidneys are small consistent with chronic medical renal disease; no hydronephrosis or acute abdominal process. Iliac vessels demonstrate mild calcified atherosclerosis but preserved caliber suitable for transplant anastomosis planning.

LVEF 55–60% with mild concentric LVH and grade I diastolic dysfunction; mild MR; no pericardial effusion. Study obtained as cardiac clearance supporting preoperative planning for renal transplant in setting of I25.10 and I10.

reports.results: CBC: Hgb 9.2 g/dL, MCV 89 fL; iron indices adequate; B12/folate normal. Impression consistent with anemia in CKD (D63.1) contributing to fatigue (R53.83) in patient with CKD stage 5/ESRD (N18.5/N18.6) on dialysis (Z99.2).
reports.notes: Obtained as part of pre-transplant workup to characterize anemia prior to requested transplant surgery.

reports.results: HIV Ag/Ab non-reactive; HBsAg non-reactive; HBsAb reactive; HCV Ab non-reactive; CMV IgG positive; EBV IgG positive.
reports.notes: Completed in preparation for requested renal transplant (CPT 50360) to inform prophylaxis and immunosuppression planning.

Over the past several months he has experienced episodes of chest tightness that are most noticeable with exertion (walking quickly between units, climbing stairs) and during periods of heightened stress. The discomfort is described as a pressure-like tightness, typically lasting a few minutes and improving with rest; on several occasions he has taken sublingual nitroglycerin with improvement. He has not undergone a stress test during this episode of care, but the persistence of symptoms has led to continued cardiovascular evaluation. His cardiovascular risk profile includes essential hypertension (I10) and hyperlipidemia (E78.5), treated with losartan and atorvastatin, along with daily low-dose aspirin for prophylaxis. Metoprolol tartrate has been used for symptom control and heart-rate moderation, and he carries Nitrostat for intermittent chest pain episodes. encounters.purpose_of_visit: Evaluation of intermittent chest pain episodes and review of recent echocardiogram results.
encounters.chief_complaint: I've been having chest pain on and off recently.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: Patient reports intermittent chest tightness over the last several weeks, sometimes with exertion and sometimes during stress. Episodes last minutes and improve with rest; denies syncope. He reviewed a recent echocardiogram performed earlier this month. 
Review of Systems: Positive for episodic chest tightness. Denies fever, cough, pleuritic pain, calf swelling, or focal neurologic deficits. 
Physical Exam: Well-appearing male, normal heart sounds without murmur, lungs clear, no edema, normal peripheral pulses. 
Assessment: Intermittent chest pain (R07.89) in the setting of hypertension (I10) and hyperlipidemia (E78.5); concern for possible coronary etiology and need for further evaluation. 
Plan: Continue aspirin and atorvastatin. Start/continue metoprolol for symptom control and prescribe PRN nitroglycerin with clear ER precautions. Continue losartan for BP control. Refer to cardiology for further assessment and consideration of anatomic coronary evaluation (CTA) given concern for coronary anomaly (Q24.5).

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: Patient presents for pre-procedure evaluation for requested cardiac CTA (CPT 75574). He reports ongoing exertional chest tightness (R07.89) several times per week with predictable triggers and relief with rest. No rest pain, syncope, or recent ER visits. He continues metoprolol and atorvastatin and attends weekly cardiac rehab.

Review of Systems: Positive for exertional chest tightness and mild exertional dyspnea with episodes. Negative for fever, cough, palpitations at rest, orthopnea, or edema.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing. CV: regular rate/rhythm, no murmurs. Respiratory: clear. Extremities: no edema.

Assessment: Patient with abnormal cardiovascular function study (R94.39) and persistent exertional chest pain (R07.89) with risk factors (I10, E78.5, Z82.49), preparing for contrast CTA. BMP confirms normal renal function.

Plan: Proceed with scheduled CTA on 04/22/2026 at UT Southwestern. Continue metoprolol with heart-rate optimization strategy; avoid caffeine and decongestants before scan. Hydration instructions reviewed. Penicillin allergy documented; no known contrast allergy. Return precautions reviewed; follow-up with cardiology after imaging results.
observations: HR near target for CTA image quality

223.procedure_requested: Insertion of tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with ESRD on hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) has recurrent AV fistula dysfunction with low flow and frequent dialysis alarms causing shortened/incomplete treatments (T82.868A). A tunneled dialysis catheter is requested to provide reliable long-term access while current access continues to fail, with peri-procedural planning considering hypertensive CKD (I12.0) and CKD anemia requiring ESA therapy (D63.1).

Objective evidence: 04-15-2026: Hgb 9.6 g/dL | 04-03-2026: Creatinine 8.9, BUN 62, CO2 19, Potassium 5.3, Hgb 9.6.
supporting_diagnoses: T82.868A - Other specified complication of vascular prosthetic devices, implants and grafts, initial encounter
I12.0 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 chronic kidney disease or end stage renal disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Hemodialysis via AV fistula created 05-15-2025 with repeated cannulation attempts and dialysis-unit flow adjustments; persistent low access flows and alarms despite these measures. Past furosemide for edema management and Mircera for anemia management (later switched).
pa_request.expected_outcome: Establishment of reliable vascular access to support completion of prescribed hemodialysis sessions with adequate blood flow and fewer interruptions.

223.bio_narrative: Caroline Channing is a 40-year-old woman living in Dallas, Texas, who has been managing end-stage renal disease (N18.6) requiring maintenance hemodialysis (Z99.2). She previously underwent AV fistula creation on 05-15-2025 and initially relied on the fistula for dialysis access. Over the last several months, she has experienced progressive difficulty completing dialysis treatments, describing frequent machine alarms, low blood-flow rates, and repeated interruptions that shortened sessions. She reports that staff often need to reposition needles and reduce pump speeds to prevent pressure alarms, but these adjustments have not prevented repeated early termination of treatments. Her symptoms around dialysis include fatigue and intermittent nausea, for which she uses ondansetron as needed. From February through April 2026, her clinical course shows persistent access-related problems across multiple encounters. At a specialist consult on 02-01-2026 she reported repeated fistula site failures leading to cut-short dialysis. During subsequent pre-op and follow-up visits (02-15-2026, 03-27-2026, 04-04-2026, and 04-09-2026), she continued to describe low fistula flow and inability to sustain adequate dialysis runs. Objective evaluation included a duplex ultrasound on 04-01-2026 demonstrating low access flow and findings concerning for a stenotic segment, consistent with vascular access complication (T82.868A). Pre-procedure labs (BMP/CBC/coagulation panel on 04-03-2026) were obtained to support planning for alternate access placement. Caroline’s comorbid conditions include hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 CKD/ESRD (I12.0), managed with amlodipine and as-needed clonidine for episodic severe elevations, and anemia of chronic kidney disease (D63.1), managed with epoetin alfa. She follows a renal-friendly, low-sodium diet and reports good medication adherence. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol sparingly at social gatherings, and has stable mental health with good support from friends. Family history is notable for hypertension in her father and type 2 diabetes in her mother. Given continued dialysis dependence and repeated fistula dysfunction documented by symptoms, dialysis-unit observations, and duplex findings, she is being referred for insertion of a tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis (CPT 36558) to provide reliable long-term access for ongoing hemodialysis.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Evaluation of recurrent AV fistula dysfunction and shortened hemodialysis sessions; access planning in ESRD on dialysis.
encounters.chief_complaint: My fistula sites keep failing and dialysis keeps getting cut short.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 40-year-old female with ESRD (N18.6) on hemodialysis (Z99.2) via AV fistula created 05-15-2025 reports increasing difficulty completing dialysis sessions over the past 6–8 weeks. Dialysis staff report frequent machine alarms and inability to maintain target blood flow, requiring repeated needle repositioning and early termination of sessions. Patient endorses fatigue after incomplete runs and occasional post-dialysis nausea controlled with ondansetron. 

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and intermittent nausea after dialysis. Denies fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, or access-site drainage. 

Physical Exam: AV fistula with weaker thrill per exam; no erythema or purulence at access sites. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Heart regular rate and rhythm. No acute distress. 

Assessment: ESRD on hemodialysis with suspected AV access dysfunction/complication (T82.868A) contributing to inadequate dialysis delivery. Hypertensive CKD with ESRD (I12.0). Anemia of CKD (D63.1) on ESA therapy. 

Plan: Order access imaging (duplex ultrasound) to evaluate flow and stenosis. Review recent labs including creatinine and anemia parameters. Continue current ESRD meds (sevelamer, renal vitamin, ESA protocol) and BP regimen (amlodipine; clonidine PRN). Discussed that alternative access such as tunneled dialysis catheter may be needed if fistula continues to fail. Return after imaging or sooner for worsening access performance.

224.bio_narrative: Ross Geller is a 57-year-old Caucasian male living in downtown San Antonio, Texas, who has been managing end stage renal disease (N18.6) with dependence on maintenance hemodialysis (Z99.2) for several years. He receives dialysis through a surgically created arteriovenous fistula (created 06-20-2023) and has remained consistent with scheduled sessions. Over time, he has continued to experience fatigue and “crash” symptoms on dialysis days, with intermittent muscle cramping and occasional post-dialysis lightheadedness. His chronic kidney disease has been accompanied by anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1), treated with epoetin alfa administered through the dialysis unit, and renal mineral-bone disorder characterized by secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin (N25.81) and abnormal phosphorus metabolism (E83.39), managed with cinacalcet and sevelamer carbonate alongside strict dietary counseling. In early 2026, his care team at Houston Methodist Hospital intensified pre-transplant planning. A renal ultrasound on 01-03-2026 documented bilaterally small echogenic kidneys with cortical thinning consistent with chronic medical renal disease, providing baseline anatomic informatio ... (truncated)

226.bio_narrative: Ross Geller is a 39-year-old male living in Houston, Texas who works in museum and field-based roles that sometimes require travel and irregular meals. Over the past several months he has experienced intermittent right-sided flank discomfort with urinary hesitancy and a reduced urinary stream. Symptoms are often accompanied by intermittent nausea and occasional vomiting (R11.2), especially during pain flares, which has affected his ability to concentrate at work and maintain his usual routine. He denies tobacco and illicit drug use and drinks alcohol minimally in social settings on weekends. He routinely walks for exercise and uses relaxation techniques when anxious during medical visits. He first sought specialty evaluation on 01-20-2026 for persistent flank pain and urinary hesitancy (R10.9, R39.11) with concern for obstruction and hydronephrosis. The working diagnoses include hydronephrosis with renal/ureteral calculous obstruction (N13.2) and unspecified hydronephrosis (N13.30). A blood test was obtained on 01-15-2026 as part of the initial workup. Medical therapy was initiated/continued with tamsulosin to facilitate stone passage, furosemide to manage perceived volume issues a ... (truncated)

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Evaluation of persistent right flank pain with urinary hesitancy and intermittent nausea.
encounters.chief_complaint: Persistent flank pain with urinary hesitancy.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: Patient reports intermittent right flank pain for several weeks with urinary hesitancy and reduced stream. Intermittent nausea without persistent vomiting; denies fever/chills. Reports increased oral fluids with limited relief.

Review of Systems: Negative for chest pain or dyspnea. GI notable for intermittent nausea. GU notable for hesitancy and decreased stream.

Physical Exam: Alert, non-toxic. Mild right CVA tenderness; abdomen soft without guarding. No suprapubic fullness.

Assessment: Concern for obstructive uropathy with hydronephrosis likely related to calculous obstruction (N13.2) and hydronephrosis (N13.30) with associated pain (R10.9), hesitancy (R39.11), and nausea (R11.2).

Plan: Continue/start tamsulosin and ondansetron PRN; start/continue furosemide. Review blood test from 01-15-2026. Discussed possible need for nephrostogram/ureterogram injection (CPT 50394) if symptoms persist. Follow up in 4 weeks.
observations: No fever or systemic signs of infection
Patient reports symptom-related activity limitation without standardized score

228.bio_narrative: Anthony Soprano is a 43-year-old male living in Houston, Texas who established care at Houston Methodist Hospital for persistent right shoulder pain that began gradually and has continued to interfere with overhead activity, lifting, and sleep. He works in a mostly sedentary role and reports that prolonged desk work and driving tend to aggravate shoulder discomfort. He initially presented on 01-05-2026 describing constant aching even at rest, and has returned for follow-up visits due to ongoing symptoms and difficulty achieving consistent relief. Over the past several months he has relied on a non-opioid regimen including diclofenac for myalgia, acetaminophen as needed for shoulder pain, and a lidocaine 5% patch applied to the painful area. Because of gastrointestinal sensitivity while using NSAIDs, he takes omeprazole daily for gastric protection. He reports completing a short course of physical therapy earlier in 2026 focused on shoulder mobility and strengthening, but he continues to report pain and functional limitation. 229.bio_narrative: Kendall Roy is a 40-year-old Caucasian male living in Austin, Texas, who has been followed by Dr. Logan Kent, MD at Austin General Healthcare for persistent low back pain that has gradually progressed to include right-sided radicular symptoms. He reports a pattern of low back pain aggravated by prolonged sitting and transitions from sitting to standing, with intermittent shooting pain down the posterior right leg and episodes of numbness/tingling. He has remained engaged in care and notes that symptoms have continued despite completing a supervised physical therapy program and maintaining a consistent home exercise routine. encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: Patient reports low back pain for several months with intermittent stiffness and episodic shooting discomfort into the right buttock. Completed a supervised PT program and continues home exercises, but still has daily pain rated 6/10 with prolonged sitting at work and driving. Denies fever, weight loss, saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder incontinence, or progressive weakness.

Review of Systems: Positive for low back pain and occasional right leg tingling. Negative for chest pain, dyspnea, abdominal pain, dysuria, and bowel/bladder changes.

Physical Exam: Mild lumbar paraspinal tenderness and reduced flexion due to pain. Strength 5/5 in bilateral lower extremities; reflexes symmetric. Straight-leg raise mildly provocative on the right. Gait steady.

Assessment: Chronic low back pain with intermittent right-sided radicular symptoms; incomplete response to PT.

Plan: Continue naproxen 500mg BID with food; discuss GI precautions. Trial of cyclobenzaprine 5mg at bedtime short-term for spasm with sedation precautions. Continue home exercise program; ergonomic counseling. Order lumbar spine imaging if worsening or persistence; return precautions reviewed.

images.findings: Five lumbar-type vertebrae. Mild disc space narrowing at L5-S1 with small anterior osteophytes. Alignment is maintained. No spondylolisthesis. Flexion/extension views show no abnormal translational movement to suggest dynamic instability. No acute fracture or destructive osseous lesion identified.

L5-S1: Right paracentral disc protrusion with annular fissure contacting the descending right S1 nerve root in the lateral recess; mild-to-moderate right neural foraminal narrowing. L4-L5: Mild broad-based disc bulge without high-grade stenosis. No significant central canal stenosis. Conus terminates normally. No epidural collection. Findings support targeted image-guided planning for the requested CT guidance for needle placement at the symptomatic level.

265.bio_narrative: Lisa Cuddy is a 57-year-old woman living in Austin, Texas, who works in healthcare administration and is accustomed to managing stress and long workdays. Over the last 7–8 months she developed increasingly frequent heartburn and burning epigastric pain (K21.9, R10.13), initially responsive to over-the-counter antacids but gradually becoming daily and worse at night. She has been consistent with lifestyle measures such as avoiding late meals and elevating the head of her bed. Despite an adequate therapeutic trial of pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily and adjunct sucralfate, she continues to have significant symptoms. Approximately 4 months before the planned procedure date, she noticed progressive difficulty swallowing solid foods (R13.10). She describes a sensation of food sticking in the mid-chest several times per week, requiring water to clear, and she has begun limiting portions to avoid discomfort. This change in intake has been accompanied by unintentional weight loss of roughly 12–14 pounds (R63.4). In the 6–8 weeks prior to gastroenterology consultation, she developed marked fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance and reported intermittent black, tarry stools (K92.1). Laboratory ... (truncated)

275.bio_narrative: Sansa Baratheon is a 70-year-old widowed woman living independently in Houston, Texas. She is soft-spoken, highly self-controlled in clinical visits, and tends to understate symptoms unless specifically asked. Over the last several months she has noticed intermittent upper abdominal discomfort that she describes as “indigestion” along with occasional nausea. The discomfort is typically epigastric (R10.13), comes and goes without a clear pattern, and is sometimes accompanied by a mild burning sensation she calls “heartburn,” consistent with a possible reflux component (K21.9). She has not reported vomiting, and her record does not document alarm symptoms such as hematemesis or melena. She has not tracked weight changes carefully and does not emphasize constitutional symptoms unless prompted. Her first contact for these symptoms was a telehealth visit in December 2025, where she described vague dyspepsia (K30) and occasional nausea (R11.0). She was advised to try dietary adjustments and intermittent OTC calcium carbonate antacid and to follow up if symptoms persisted. At a primary care office visit in February 2026, symptoms were again discussed; the plan focused on basic laboratory ... (truncated)

274.bio_narrative: Ann Perkins is a 69-year-old divorced woman living in Austin, Texas, who is known for being highly organized and proactive about preventive care. She previously worked in public-facing administrative roles and remains active in her community, often coordinating appointments and health tasks for friends and family. Over the past two months, she developed frequent epigastric burning discomfort with reflux symptoms, including sour taste and regurgitation, worse with coffee and late meals. She also noticed fatigue and reduced stamina during her usual walks. She denied hematemesis and did not clearly observe melena, but her primary care physician obtained stool testing due to concern for occult blood loss. On 2026-03-12, her FIT/FOBT returned positive, documented as R19.5 (Other fecal abnormalities). A follow-up laboratory evaluation on 2026-03-14 demonstrated microcytic anemia with low ferritin and low transferrin saturation, consistent with D50.9 (Iron deficiency anemia, unspecified). Her symptoms persisted despite initiation of pantoprazole and focused lifestyle measures. A key contributing risk factor is her long-term NSAID exposure (Z79.1) related to chronic right knee osteoarthrit ... (truncated)

273.bio_narrative: Severus Finch is a 64-year-old divorced man living in Houston, Texas, who presents as reserved and analytical, preferring to keep his health concerns private until symptoms persist. He recently established care with a new primary care physician after relocating within the city to be closer to a small support network. He is meticulous with routines and tends to self-manage minor symptoms with over-the-counter remedies before seeking medical advice. Over the past several months, he has noticed intermittent changes in bowel habits (R19.4) characterized by episodic constipation (K59.00) and variable stool frequency. He describes periods of one to two days without a bowel movement followed by smaller, less satisfying stools, without a clearly identified dietary trigger. Initial primary care evaluation focused on general counseling regarding hydration and fiber intake, with intermittent polyethylene glycol use discussed; no defined course of a structured bowel regimen is documented. Basic laboratory testing (CBC/CMP) was obtained and did not reveal anemia or other specific abnormalities, but targeted GI testing (e.g., inflammatory markers, stool studies) and abdominal imaging are not pre ... 272.bio_narrative: Monica Geller is a 61-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas, who is meticulous about routines and tends to push through symptoms until they disrupt her day. Over the past 5–6 months she noticed a persistent change in bowel habits (R19.4), alternating between constipation with straining and episodes of looser stools. During this period she also began seeing intermittent dark stools and occasional red blood mixed with stool (K92.1). She initially attributed symptoms to stress and diet changes, but over time she developed progressive fatigue and an unintentional weight loss (R63.4), prompting her to seek care. At her primary care visit on 2026-02-18, she described ongoing cramping and bloating with intermittent bleeding. Conservative measures were started, including a bowel regimen with polyethylene glycol and diet counseling, and she used a short course of rectal hydrocortisone for suspected hemorrhoidal irritation (past use). Given the persistence and pattern of bleeding, her PCP ordered a fecal immunochemical test and laboratory evaluation. On 2026-03-06, FIT returned positive and CBC showed a mild microcytic anemia pattern consistent with iron deficiency (D50.9). A CT abdomen/pe ... (truncated)

The abdominal exam was benign, and initial laboratory testing (CBC, CMP, TSH) was ordered; results were within reference ranges. 264.bio_narrative: Carmela S. is a 59-year-old woman living in Dallas, Texas, followed by Dr. Marisol Vega at UT Southwestern Family Medicine at Park Cities. Over the past several months she has described intermittent post-prandial burning in the upper abdomen (R10.13) with a pattern consistent with dyspepsia (K30) and reflux symptoms (K21.9). She notes that symptoms tend to fluctuate with meal timing and the types of foods she eats, especially late-night meals and caffeinated beverages. She also experiences intermittent nausea (R11.0) and uses ondansetron ODT only when symptoms are bothersome. She has not reported gastrointestinal bleeding, persistent vomiting, progressive swallowing difficulty, or other alarm features in her visits documented to date. Her symptom management has largely been episodic. She uses famotidine 20 mg and calcium carbonate antacids as needed, often waiting until discomfort is noticeable before taking medication. She avoids NSAIDs after noticing upper GI upset with ibuprofen in the past and prefers acetaminophen for occasional musculoskeletal pain. 267.bio_narrative: Anthony Stark is a 64-year-old divorced male living in Houston, Texas who works as an engineering consultant and spends long hours at a desk with frequent travel around the metro area. He describes himself as highly driven and detail-oriented, often pushing through symptoms until they become disruptive. Over the last 4–6 months he noted intermittent bright red blood per rectum, typically streaking the stool or on toilet paper, occurring several times per month. During the same period he developed a change in bowel habits with alternating constipation and looser stools and recurrent left lower quadrant cramping discomfort that improves somewhat after bowel movements. He denies fever or acute infectious symptoms but reports increasing concern because of his family history and prior colonoscopy findings. He has a documented personal history of colonic polyps (Z86.010) with a prior colonoscopy in 2022 that included removal of adenomatous polyp(s) and recommendation for surveillance. He also has a confirmed high-risk family history of colorectal cancer in a first-degree relative (Z80.0), which he reports as his father being diagnosed in his early 60s. images.findings: CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast performed for prior episode of left lower quadrant pain. No acute inflammatory process identified; no diverticulitis visualized. Colon demonstrates moderate stool burden without obstruction. No free air or free fluid. Findings were used to support conservative management at that time and are referenced in current workup to contextualize recurrent LLQ discomfort (R10.32) while planning diagnostic colonoscopy (CPT 45378).

reports.results: WBC 6.4 K/µL; Hgb 13.6 g/dL; Hct 41.0%; MCV 81 fL; Platelets 268 K/µL. No leukocytosis; hemoglobin within reference range with low-normal MCV trend. Results support procedural planning and help assess for anemia in the context of reported hematochezia (K62.5) prior to requested colonoscopy (CPT 45378).
reports.notes: Patient advised to continue monitoring bleeding; instructed on ER precautions. CBC obtained as part of pre-colonoscopy evaluation for persistent bleeding symptoms.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Recurrent exertional chest pressure with dyspnea (R07.9, R06.02) in a 69-year-old male with hypertension (I10), diabetes (E11.9), and hyperlipidemia (E78.5). Myocardial perfusion SPECT (CPT 78452) is requested to assess for inducible ischemia and to guide outpatient medical management planning.

Objective evidence: 03-09-2026: Creatinine 0.98, eGFR 85, Hgb 14.6.
supporting_diagnoses: E11.9 - Type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications
M17.11 - Unilateral primary osteoarthritis, right knee
pa_request.previous_treatments: Beta-blocker therapy, calcium-channel blocker therapy, aspirin therapy, statin therapy, and PRN nitroglycerin; lifestyle modification counseling and prior completion of cardiac rehabilitation.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Objective ischemia risk stratification and characterization of perfusion to support treatment planning and follow-up decisions.

210.bio_narrative: Oscar Martinez is a 69-year-old Hispanic male living in Dallas, Texas, followed in primary care by Dr. Karen Fields, MD at UT Southwestern Medical Center. He has longstanding essential hypertension (I10), type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications (E11.9), and hyperlipidemia (E78.5). Over the past several weeks he developed intermittent tight chest pressure with exertion, most noticeable when climbing stairs or walking quickly, along with shortness of breath (R07.9, R06.02). The symptoms generally improve with rest and have led him to reduce activity. He has right knee osteoarthritis (M17.11), which limits walking distance and makes sustained treadmill exercise difficult. His symptoms prompted an office visit on 03-12-2026 and an ER evaluation on 03-18-2026 for chest tightness and dyspnea. During the ER visit he had a chest X-ray and lab evaluation including high-sensitivity troponin testing; no acute cardiopulmonary process was recorded on imaging and there was no biochemical evidence of acute myocardial injury. Given continued exertional symptoms and multiple ASCVD risk factors, he was referred to cardiology for outpatient risk stratification. He has been using metoprolol succinate ER for blood pressure and symptom control, amlodipine for hypertension, aspirin for cardiovascular risk reduction in the setting of chest pain evaluation, and sublingual nitroglycerin as needed for chest pressure. He remains interested in understanding what the testing means and how it will inform medication planning. Socially, he reports being highly organized with medications and health logs, lives with his spouse, and remains engaged in care. He reports social alcohol use and denies tobacco use and illicit drugs. He describes exercising 5 days per week with moderate to vigorous walking and light resistance training when his knee allows, using home exercises from prior physical therapy. Family history is notable for coronary artery disease in his father (myocardial infarction in his early 60s) and type 2 diabetes in multiple first-degree relatives. After cardiology consultation on 03-25-2026, a transthoracic echocardiogram (CPT 93306) on 04-01-2026 showed preserved left ventricular systolic function with mild concentric hypertrophy and Grade I diastolic dysfunction. With persistent exertional symptoms and baseline echo available, myocardial perfusion imaging by SPECT (CPT 78452) is requested to evaluate for inducible ischemia and provide objective perfusion and functional information to guide ongoing outpatient management.

211.bio_narrative: Maxine Porter is a 66-year-old woman living in the Heights area of Houston, Texas, who works long shifts in a busy customer-facing role. Over the last several months she has noticed a recurring pattern of substernal tightness and a “winded” feeling when she is rushing between tasks, carrying items, or climbing stairs. The discomfort is exertional rather than at rest and is often paired with shortness of breath (R06.02). She initially wondered if the sensation might be reflux or stress-related, but episodes have continued during physical activity despite a past trial of omeprazole, which did not prevent the exertional events. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol only socially (about 1–2 drinks per month), and denies illicit drug use. Her diet is inconsistent during late shifts and tends to be higher sodium, although she has started packing meals twice weekly. Her medical history includes essential hypertension (I10), hyperlipidemia (E78.5), and obesity (E66.9), which increase cardiovascular risk in the setting of exertional symptoms. On 03-11-2026 she presented to primary care reporting exertional chest tightness and dyspnea (R07.89, R06.02). A 12-lead ECG (CPT 93000) demonstrated sinus rhythm with nonspecific ST-T abnormalities, providing a baseline that can limit specificity of subsequent exercise ECG interpretation. On 03-24-2026 she went to the ER for a longer-than-usual episode of chest pressure with dyspnea; chest X-ray (2 views) did not show an acute pulmonary process. She subsequently had a cardiology consultation on 04-08-2026 where guideline-directed risk reduction and symptom-directed therapy were reviewed and continued, including beta-blocker therapy, aspirin, statin, and PRN nitroglycerin. Her family history is notable for premature cardiovascular disease risk patterns: her father had a myocardial infarction in his early 60s and her mother was treated for longstanding hypertension. Maxine reports heightened worry when chest symptoms occur at work and is engaged in active psychotherapy every two weeks, practicing breathing exercises to manage symptom-related anxiety. On 04-15-2026 she completed an exercise treadmill stress test (CPT 93015) that reproduced exertional symptoms but yielded equivocal ECG changes in the context of baseline ST-T abnormalities. Given ongoing exertional chest pain and shortness of breath despite medical therapy and persistent risk factors, her cardiologist requested myocardial perfusion imaging with SPECT (CPT 78452) to assess for inducible ischemia and to guide next-step management planning.

220.bio_narrative: April Ludgate is a 69-year-old woman living in Austin, Texas, followed in primary care at UT Austin Dell Medical School Clinic. Over the last several years she has lived with long-standing hypertension and type 2 diabetes, with progressive chronic kidney disease that has now reached end stage renal disease (N18.6). She has become increasingly symptomatic since early 2026, describing persistent nausea with intermittent vomiting (R11.2), profound fatigue limiting routine activities (R53.83), and diffuse itching (L29.9). She also reports episodic lower-extremity swelling (R60.0), and her outpatient course has included repeated medication adjustments to manage electrolyte and acid-base complications typical of advanced kidney failure, including recurrent hyperkalemia history (E87.5) and metabolic acidosis (E87.2). Her medication regimen reflects management of ESRD complications and comorbidities: sevelamer carbonate (Renvela) for phosphorus/mineral balance, patiromer (Veltassa) for hyperkalemia history, sodium bicarbonate for acidosis, furosemide for volume management, and amlodipine for blood pressure control in hypertensive CKD with ESRD (I12.0). Diabetes management includes nightly insulin glargine, and she takes oral iron for anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1). Lisinopril was previously used for hypertension/renal protection but was stopped after episodes of hyperkalemia as kidney function declined. April’s recent clinical timeline includes an office visit on 02-18-2026 for escalating fatigue and nausea, an ER visit on 03-10-2026 for weakness and GI symptoms, and subsequent outpatient diagnostic workup and dialysis preparation. Imaging includes a renal ultrasound on 03-18-2026 showing chronic medical renal disease and vascular access mapping on 03-25-2026 to support hemodialysis access planning. Additional pre-procedure testing included a chest X-ray and baseline ECG on 04-08-2026 and hepatitis B serology on 04-09-2026; she received hepatitis B vaccination as part of dialysis readiness. Socially, she is a former smoker with rare alcohol use and no illicit drug use. She relies on her partner for transportation, and her guarded, skeptical affect has been most notable around scheduling and care coordination. She is engaged in renal diet counseling every two weeks and prefers structured meal plans; however, nausea and fatigue sometimes lead to missed meals, which can shift medication timing. Family history is notable for diabetes and hypertension, aligning with her comorbidity burden. The current referral requests initiation of dialysis (CPT 90999) with an anticipated start date in late May 2026, following documented ESRD complications and persistent symptoms, while ongoing care coordination continues to formalize longitudinal nephrology supervision and the finalized dialysis plan-of-care documentation.

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 69-year-old female with ESRD (N18.6) in the context of hypertensive CKD (I12.0) and diabetic CKD (E11.22) referred for hemodialysis access planning. Patient endorses ongoing nausea (R11.2), fatigue (R53.83), and pruritus (L29.9) consistent with uremic symptoms. She has had intermittent edema (R60.0) responsive to diuretic therapy. She reports adherence to phosphate binder, potassium binder, and bicarbonate regimen.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue, nausea, itching. Negative for active infection symptoms.

Physical Exam: Upper extremities without cellulitis; palpable radial pulses bilaterally. No focal neurologic deficits.

Assessment: ESRD (N18.6) with need for dialysis access planning. Comorbid diabetes with CKD (E11.22) and hypertensive CKD with ESRD (I12.0) increase vascular risk, making preoperative mapping important.

Plan: Reviewed upper extremity venous/arterial mapping ultrasound completed today; anatomy appears suitable for creation of arteriovenous access. Discussed access options, typical maturation timeline, and infection prevention. Coordinated with primary team for ongoing pre-dialysis lab monitoring and dialysis unit requirements (including hepatitis B screening and vaccination status).
observations: No signs of active skin infection at potential access sites.
Patient focused on scheduling timeline and next steps.

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 69-year-old female with ESRD (N18.6) returns for pre-procedure evaluation and care coordination prior to dialysis start planning. She reports continued nausea with intermittent vomiting (R11.2), pruritus (L29.9) with excoriations, and fatigue (R53.83). She notes intermittent ankle edema (R60.0) controlled with furosemide. She reports taking medications as scheduled but meal skipping due to nausea affects binder timing. She asks for clear scheduling steps for dialysis initiation and ongoing supervising clinician.

Review of Systems: Positive for nausea, itching, fatigue, intermittent edema. Negative for fever or chest pain.

Physical Exam: Appears fatigued; skin with excoriations on forearms; no cellulitis. Lungs clear. Heart regular. Abdomen benign. 1+ ankle edema.

Assessment: ESRD (N18.6) with persistent symptomatic uremia (R11.2, L29.9, R53.83) and intermittent edema (R60.0), with comorbid hypertensive CKD/ESRD (I12.0), diabetes with CKD (E11.22), anemia in CKD (D63.1), metabolic acidosis (E87.2), and hyperkalemia history (E87.5). Pre-procedure testing (CXR and ECG) completed 04-08-2026 and hepatitis B serology completed 04-09-2026 in preparation for dialysis initiation.

Plan: Reviewed current medications and reinforced meal planning strategies with active renal diet therapy. Continue sevelamer, patiromer, sodium bicarbonate, furosemide, amlodipine, insulin glargine, and ferrous sulfate. Discussed dialysis start target date and the need to finalize dialysis unit intake and ensure an identified supervising nephrologist for ongoing dialysis orders. Reviewed allergy list (penicillin, shellfish). Provided return precautions for worsening symptoms or shortness of breath.
observations: Patient expresses anxiety about scheduling logistics and oversight.

images.findings: Ultrasound demonstrates bilaterally increased renal cortical echogenicity with cortical thinning, compatible with chronic medical renal disease. Kidneys are reduced in size compared with expected adult norms, without hydronephrosis. No obstructing stone is visualized. Urinary bladder is partially distended without focal mass. Findings support chronic kidney disease progression consistent with ESRD (N18.6) and were obtained as part of diagnostic workup prior to dialysis planning.

images.facility: Ascension Seton Medical Center Austin - Vascular Lab
images.findings: Bilateral upper extremity arterial and venous mapping performed for hemodialysis access planning prior to dialysis initiation. Cephalic and basilic veins are patent without thrombus; diameters are adequate for access creation in the nondominant upper extremity with compressibility preserved. Radial and brachial arteries demonstrate triphasic flow and no significant stenosis. No evidence of central venous obstruction on limited evaluation. Findings support feasibility of planned dialysis access pathway for ESRD (N18.6).

images.facility: Ascension Seton Medical Center Austin - Radiology
images.findings: Two-view chest radiograph obtained as baseline cardiopulmonary assessment in preparation for upcoming dialysis initiation and access-related procedures. Cardiomediastinal silhouette is within upper limits of normal. Mild interstitial prominence is present without focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. No acute osseous abnormality is seen. Imaging provides baseline evaluation of volume status and cardiopulmonary condition in ESRD (N18.6) with intermittent edema (R60.0).

reports.results: Key abnormalities consistent with advanced renal failure physiology in ESRD (N18.6): creatinine markedly elevated; BUN elevated; bicarbonate low consistent with metabolic acidosis (E87.2). Potassium elevated/borderline high consistent with hyperkalemia history (E87.5). CBC notable for anemia consistent with anemia in CKD (D63.1). Glucose elevated consistent with diabetes with CKD (E11.22).
reports.notes: Obtained during ER visit for weakness and nausea (R11.2) as part of pre-dialysis workup to assess electrolyte and acid-base derangements; findings prompted expedited outpatient dialysis planning and continued potassium binder and bicarbonate therapy.

reports.results: Persistent ESRD-associated biochemical pattern: decreased eGFR consistent with ESRD (N18.6); low bicarbonate consistent with ongoing acidosis (E87.2) on sodium bicarbonate therapy; phosphorus elevated with ongoing binder use; hemoglobin low consistent with anemia in CKD (D63.1) with iron indices supporting continued oral iron. Potassium controlled relative to prior values with ongoing patiromer therapy, consistent with treated hyperkalemia history (E87.5).
reports.notes: Collected to trend metabolic status and anemia/mineral balance prior to dialysis initiation. Results were used to continue and reinforce phosphate binder, potassium binder, alkali therapy, and iron supplementation and to support escalation to dialysis planning given persistent symptoms and lab derangements.

reports.results: Hepatitis B surface antigen: negative. Hepatitis B core antibody (total): negative. Hepatitis B surface antibody: low-positive/indeterminate range after catch-up vaccination, consistent with early post-vaccination response; recommended to complete vaccine series and recheck per dialysis unit protocol.
reports.notes: Obtained as part of dialysis unit infection prevention requirements and pre-dialysis onboarding. Vaccination dated 03-12-2026 documented; serology supports ongoing completion/monitoring prior to dialysis start.

pa_request.clinical_justification: Patient reports persistent functional impairment with chewing fatigue, inefficient mastication, and diet texture restriction associated with Class II malocclusion. pa_request.previous_treatments: Medical management for nasal obstruction with intranasal corticosteroid and saline spray; symptomatic analgesics and diet modification; ongoing orthodontic surgical preparation.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Improved occlusal alignment and masticatory function with decreased chewing fatigue and improved diet tolerance; improved perioperative planning for airway management in the setting of chronic mouth breathing and sleep-related symptoms.

230.bio_narrative: April Ludgate is a 68-year-old woman living in downtown Austin, Texas, who has historically minimized symptoms until they interfere with daily routines. Over the past 1–2 years she has noticed increasing difficulty chewing and maintaining endurance through a typical meal. She reports that foods requiring sustained grinding (steak, raw vegetables, crusty bread) lead to jaw tightness and fatigue, and she frequently pauses mid-meal or chooses softer textures. This pattern is associated with masticatory muscle soreness that she manages with intermittent analgesics. She describes the problem as functional rather than cosmetic and reports that it affects meal pacing, social dining, and nutrition variety. She also reports chronic nasal obstruction with routine mouth breathing and dry mouth on waking. The nasal symptoms fluctuate but are present most days, and she uses daily intranasal fluticasone and saline spray. Snoring and non-restorative sleep are noted in the record, and she uses melatonin at bedtime when sleep initiation is difficult. She has intermittent throat irritation and a sensation of swallowing effort during periods of reflux symptoms; she takes omeprazole daily which she fe ... (truncated)

Cephalometric analysis and CBCT demonstrate maxillary hypoplasia and jaw relationship abnormality correlating with impaired chewing endurance and jaw pain/TMJ symptoms. Controlled essential hypertension is incorporated into perioperative planning for the requested Le Fort I osteotomy (CPT 21141).
supporting_diagnoses: K07.0 - Major anomalies of jaw size
pa_request.previous_treatments: Occlusal guard (completed), topical diclofenac, acetaminophen, past ibuprofen course stopped due to gastritis, diet modification, active physical therapy, and comprehensive orthodontic fixed appliances; symptoms and functional limitation persist despite these measures.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Improved occlusal relationship and masticatory function with better chewing endurance and decreased abnormal muscle loading contributing to jaw pain; stable postoperative occlusion to support continued orthodontic finishing.

231.bio_narrative: Arya Sandoval is a 68-year-old woman living in Fort Worth, Texas who has remained active in her household and community but has gradually limited her diet and social meals due to increasing chewing fatigue and jaw pain. Over the last several years she noticed her bite becoming harder to “find,” with the sensation that her teeth do not meet evenly. During the past 12–18 months, she has developed predictable aching in the jaw and temples by the end of the day, especially after prolonged meals, and she avoids tougher foods because chewing endurance drops and soreness builds. She describes intermittent tenderness over the jaw muscles and TMJ region with a sense of overwork rather than a single acute injury. These symptoms are consistent with jaw pain (R68.84) and temporomandibular joint disorder symptoms (M26.609) in the setting of a malocclusion. She has pursued stepwise conservative care. A night occlusal guard was fabricated and fitted on 2025-11-18, which reduced morning tightness but did not restore comfortable chewing function. For pain flares she uses acetaminophen and topical diclofenac to the jaw/temple area; she previously used ibuprofen for short periods but stopped when it ... (truncated)

222.procedure_requested: Insertion of tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with advanced CKD and progressive uremic symptoms is being prepared for hemodialysis access. A tunneled central venous catheter is requested to provide reliable access for initiation of renal replacement therapy while access planning continues. Comorbid hypertensive CKD and anemia of CKD are present and complicate fluid and blood pressure management.

Objective evidence: 03-24-2026: Creatinine 4.1 mg/dL, BUN 62 mg/dL, CO2 18 mmol/L, Potassium 5.2 mmol/L, Hgb 9.4 g/dL.
supporting_diagnoses: I12.9 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 1 through stage 4 chronic kidney disease, or unspecified chronic kidney disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Dietary sodium restriction and fluid guidance, loop diuretic therapy, antihypertensive management, alkali therapy for acidosis, iron supplementation, and antiemetic as needed for nausea.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Dependable vascular access to allow initiation of hemodialysis as clinically scheduled, supporting volume and symptom management and enabling ongoing laboratory and medication management.

222.bio_narrative: Dina Fox is a 60-year-old woman in Austin, Texas who has been followed in primary care at UT Health Austin for hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.9) with progression to chronic kidney disease stage 4 (N18.4). Over the last year she has noticed a gradual decline in stamina with worsening fatigue (R53.83) that now interferes with long workdays. Since early 2026 she has also had intermittent nausea (R11.0), reduced appetite on some days, and difficulty maintaining consistent diet choices due to variable shifts. She prefers clear, rule-based instructions and uses structured routines, including a weekly pill organizer, to manage a complex regimen. In March 2026, Dina presented to clinic reporting exhaustion and higher home blood pressure readings. She continued amlodipine and furosemide for volume and blood pressure management and remained on sodium bicarbonate for CKD-associated acidosis (E87.2). On 03-24-2026 she went to the emergency department for nausea and profound fatigue; peripheral IV access was difficult, reinforcing the practical challenges of venous access as her CKD care intensifies. Subsequent nephrology consultation focused on planning for renal replacement therapy and coordinating pre-procedure evaluation steps. A renal ultrasound (03-31-2026) showed chronic medical renal disease without hydronephrosis, supporting chronicity rather than a reversible obstructive cause. Upper extremity venous mapping (04-04-2026) was performed as part of access planning. Her medication history includes a prior trial of lisinopril (Prinivil) that was stopped after concern for worsening renal function and mild hyperkalemia. She also takes ferrous sulfate for anemia in CKD (D63.1) and uses ondansetron as needed for nausea. She has been taking apixaban (Eliquis) during periods of reduced mobility and access planning; coagulation labs were checked on 04-12-2026 to support procedural planning. Given progressive symptoms in the setting of advanced CKD and the need for reliable access for initiation of hemodialysis, placement of a tunneled central venous dialysis catheter (CPT 36558) is scheduled at Ascension Seton Medical Center Austin on 05-20-2026. Socially, Dina is a former smoker and reports rare alcohol use. She denies illicit drug use. She is engaged in mental health psychotherapy every two weeks to manage elevated stress and irritability around uncertainty in her medical plan. Family history is notable for hypertension and kidney disease in a first-degree relative, which she reports has influenced her motivation to follow through with access planning and structured follow-up.

pa_request.clinical_justification: Patient with advanced chronic kidney disease with progressive fatigue, intermittent nausea, and declining renal function is being prepared for initiation of hemodialysis. A tunneled central venous catheter is requested to establish reliable vascular access and enable timely outpatient dialysis start while long-term access planning continues. Comorbid hypertensive chronic kidney disease increases the importance of dependable access and minimizing repeated peripheral IV attempts.
supporting_diagnoses: I12.0 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 chronic kidney disease or end stage renal disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Blood pressure management with amlodipine and carvedilol; phosphate binding with sevelamer with meals; iron supplementation for CKD anemia; vitamin D supplementation; ondansetron as needed for nausea. Prior intermittent furosemide use for swelling was discontinued.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Establish central venous access to support planned hemodialysis with improved treatment logistics and reliable vascular access.

2221.bio_narrative: Amelia Shepherd is a 42-year-old woman in Dallas, Texas who has been followed in primary care at UT Southwestern for hypertension that has gradually progressed into hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.0). Over the last several years she has noticed a slow decline in energy and increasing difficulty making it through long work shifts. By late 2025, her nephrology workup included a renal ultrasound (completed 2025-09-18), and she began to focus more on diet and blood pressure adherence as her kidney function worsened. In early 2026, she reported persistent fatigue (R53.83) that became daily and began to affect concentration and sleep. She also developed intermittent nausea (R11.0), often worse when she skipped meals or ate higher-sodium convenience foods during busy days. She has been managed medically with antihypertensives (amlodipine and carvedilol) and CKD-directed supportive therapy including sevelamer with meals to support phosphate control and vitamin D supplementation. She takes oral iron (ferrous sulfate) for anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1) and uses ondansetron as needed for nausea. She previously used low-dose furosemide intermittently for swelling during CKD ... (truncated)

2231.bio_narrative: Severus Finch is a 66-year-old man living in Dallas, Texas who has been managing progressive hypertensive chronic kidney disease that advanced to end-stage renal disease (I12.0, N18.6). He is dialysis-dependent (Z99.2) and has structured his week around thrice-weekly hemodialysis and medication routines. He keeps written checklists for medications and dialysis-day tasks, and he relies on his sibling for occasional transportation and appointment reminders when fatigue is worse. His current referral is driven by repeated loss of adequate dialysis access performance from his left upper extremity AV graft. The graft was created on 06-18-2025 and later required thrombectomy with revision on 11-14-2025 after thrombosis. Starting in early 2026, he began noticing that during dialysis the staff frequently had to decrease pump speeds due to low flow and alarms, and he was often taken off early. He reported feeling more fatigued and “washed out” after shortened runs, with intermittent swelling between treatments and concern about not receiving the full prescribed dialysis time. A hemodialysis access duplex ultrasound on 04-01-2026 demonstrated low access flow and a hemodynamically significant ... (truncated)

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with ESRD on hemodialysis has recurrent access malfunction with repeated alarms and shortened treatments, including an ED visit for volume overload after incomplete dialysis. Due to dialysis dependence and access mechanical complication, a tunneled dialysis catheter is requested to provide reliable vascular access while access management continues. Heart failure and hypertension increase risk when dialysis sessions are interrupted.

Objective evidence: 04-15-2026: Creatinine 9.1, BUN 62, CO2 20, Potassium 5.4, Hgb 9.4 g/dL | 04-15-2026: Potassium 5.4 mmol/L, Hgb 9.4 g/dL.
supporting_diagnoses: T82.590A - Other mechanical complication of other vascular grafts, initial encounter
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Dialysis unit troubleshooting (repositioning, cannulation changes, reduced flow trials) with persistent interruptions; antiplatelet therapy during access dysfunction evaluation; short past course Lokelma for hyperkalemia; AV fistula creation 03-15-2022.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Reliable vascular access to allow completion of prescribed hemodialysis treatments and reduced risk of recurrent volume overload episodes.

113.bio_narrative: Corrado Soprano is a 73-year-old man living in downtown Dallas who has been managing progressive kidney disease for several years and transitioned to end stage renal disease (N18.6) requiring chronic hemodialysis with dialysis dependence (Z99.2). He receives care through UT Southwestern Kidney Clinic with Dr. Marcus Alvarez, MD. A left radiocephalic arteriovenous fistula was created on 03-15-2022 and served as his primary access, but over the last 6–8 weeks he has experienced increasing difficulty completing treatments due to recurrent alarms and inadequate access flow, consistent with a mechanical complication of dialysis access (T82.590A). He reports fatigue after shortened sessions and notes swelling that builds between treatments when ultrafiltration targets cannot be met. In early April, after a dialysis session ended early because the access repeatedly alarmed, he developed shortness of breath and worsening leg swelling, prompting an ER visit on 04-03-2026. Evaluation included chest X-ray findings consistent with pulmonary vascular congestion in the setting of chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32), and he required urgent hemodialysis. Despite repeated dialysis-unit interventions (needle repositioning, alternate cannulation sites, and reduced flow trials), he continues to have interrupted treatments, and objective vascular access evaluation with duplex ultrasound on 04-02-2026 supported ongoing access dysfunction. Pre-op labs on 04-15-2026 documented anemia of chronic kidney disease (D63.1) and an ESRD chemistry profile, including mild hyperkalemia (E87.5), reinforcing the need for consistent dialysis clearance. He takes amlodipine and carvedilol for blood pressure and heart failure management (I10, I50.32) and receives standard ESRD medications including phosphate binder and calcitriol. He remains generally adherent with dialysis attendance and medications, though he describes occasional dietary lapses at social events. He is a former smoker and drinks alcohol only on special occasions. Family history is notable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease in first-degree relatives. Given repeated inability to complete prescribed dialysis and the clinical consequences of interrupted treatments, he is being referred for insertion of a tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis (CPT 36558) to provide reliable access while longer-term management of his fistula is addressed.

encounters.chief_complaint: My dialysis access keeps alarming and I can't finish treatments like I used to.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 73-year-old male with ESRD (N18.6) on chronic hemodialysis (Z99.2) via left radiocephalic AV fistula reports 4–6 weeks of increasing dialysis machine alarms and shortened treatments due to poor flows. He notes more fatigue after dialysis days and occasional cramping when ultrafiltration is reduced. Denies fever or access site drainage. He reports blood pressure has been running higher on non-dialysis days.

ROS: Positive for intermittent dyspnea on exertion and lower extremity swelling when dialysis sessions are shortened. Negative for chest pain, syncope, cough, and chills.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. Cardiovascular: regular rate/rhythm. Lungs: faint bibasilar crackles. Extremities: 1+ bilateral ankle edema. Left forearm AV fistula with palpable thrill but intermittently diminished compared with prior visits; no erythema, warmth, or drainage.

Assessment: ESRD on dialysis with suspected mechanical access complication (T82.590A) based on repeated alarms/incomplete treatments; hypertension (I10); chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32) with volume sensitivity; anemia in CKD (D63.1) managed per protocol.

Plan: Order duplex ultrasound of left upper extremity hemodialysis access. Coordinate with dialysis unit for detailed flow/pressure logs. Continue current BP regimen (amlodipine, carvedilol) and reinforce dry weight goals. Continue Renvela, calcitriol, and epoetin protocol. Review access precautions and advise ER precautions for worsening dyspnea/edema.
observations: Dialysis sessions reported shortened due to alarms and poor flows.
Left AV fistula thrill present but variable by exam.

images.type: Duplex Ultrasound - Left Upper Extremity Hemodialysis Access
images.findings: Duplex evaluation supports left radiocephalic AV fistula dysfunction with turbulent flow and elevated velocities near the anastomosis/outflow segments consistent with hemodynamically significant narrowing. No complete occlusion or large pseudoaneurysm is identified on this study, though outflow vein irregularity correlates with difficult cannulation and intermittent inadequate flows reported during dialysis. Findings are consistent with mechanical complication of dialysis access (T82.590A) in the setting of ESRD (N18.6) and dialysis dependence (Z99.2).

images.provider: ED Physician - UT Southwestern Medical Center
images.findings: Two-view chest radiographs show mild cardiomegaly with pulmonary vascular congestion and mild interstitial prominence. Small bilateral pleural effusions with mild basilar atelectasis are present; no focal consolidation or pneumothorax. Findings correlate with volume overload after shortened dialysis and are consistent with chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32) in a patient with ESRD (N18.6), supporting the need for reliable dialysis access.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with advanced chronic kidney disease with progressive fatigue and recurrent electrolyte abnormalities despite medication optimization. Tunneled central venous catheter placement is requested to establish reliable access for planned hemodialysis initiation; comorbid hypertensive chronic kidney disease and anemia in CKD increase risk of decompensation without dependable access.

Objective evidence: 03-31-2026: Creatinine 6.2 mg/dL, BUN 74 mg/dL, CO2 18 mmol/L, Potassium 5.4 mmol/L, Hgb 9.4 g/dL.
supporting_diagnoses: I12.0 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 chronic kidney disease or end stage renal disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Amlodipine for BP control; furosemide for volume management; sodium zirconium cyclosilicate for hyperkalemia risk reduction; sevelamer carbonate for phosphorus control; epoetin alfa and oral iron for anemia support; renal diet counseling.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Establish durable central venous access for initiation of outpatient hemodialysis with improved ability to manage volume and electrolyte balance.

112.bio_narrative: Jesse Pinkman is a 58-year-old man living in Dallas, Texas who has been followed by Dr. Daniel Kim at UT Southwestern Internal Medicine Clinic for hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.0). Over the past year he has noticed worsening day-to-day fatigue and reduced stamina at work and home. He reports that his blood pressure has been difficult to keep within goal ranges and that missed doses occur when his schedule changes, although he has worked on being more consistent since an emergency visit earlier this year. In October 2025 he presented to primary care with persistent tiredness and elevated blood pressure, leading to medication optimization including amlodipine. By early February 2026 he had an ER visit for generalized weakness and muscle cramping; the episode was treated as an electrolyte-related event in the setting of advanced kidney disease, and he was started on sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (Lokelma) afterward to reduce hyperkalemia risk (E87.5). Subsequent nephrology evaluation noted continued symptoms affecting function, and he was started on a CKD-focused medication regimen including furosemide (Lasix) for trace edema/volume management and sevelamer carbonate (Renvela) for dietary phosphorus control as renal dysfunction progressed (N18.6). He also has anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1) managed with epoetin alfa (Epogen) injections and ferrous sulfate, which has helped maintain therapy continuity but has not eliminated fatigue. He completed a brief nutrition counseling series with a dietitian and has attempted to follow low-potassium and low-phosphorus guidance, though he struggles with meal planning and often relies on processed foods. He has remained engaged in weekly physical therapy to preserve conditioning while navigating the limitations imposed by advanced kidney disease. Family history is notable for hypertension and kidney disease in a first-degree relative per patient report. Socially, he is a former smoker and drinks alcohol socially on weekends; he denies current illicit drug use. He reports irritability and sleep disruption related to health stress, and he tends to minimize symptoms until they interfere with daily activities. Given progressive symptoms, recurrent electrolyte vulnerability, and ongoing dialysis planning, the care team is coordinating pre-procedure evaluation and scheduling for insertion of a tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis (CPT 36558) at UT Southwestern Medical Center. The goal is to establish dependable central access to support planned hemodialysis initiation while ongoing CKD management continues.

pa_request.clinical_justification: Patient has nephrostomy tube in place for hydronephrosis/ureteral calculous obstruction with ongoing concerns about drainage and intermittent flank discomfort. CPT 50394 is requested to evaluate collecting system and ureteral patency via contrast injection through the existing nephrostomy tube to guide catheter management planning. Comorbid morbid obesity and hypertension are considered in procedural planning and peri-procedural monitoring.

pa_request.expected_outcome: Anatomic and functional clarification of collecting system and ureteral patency to guide nephrostomy tube management decisions.

116.bio_narrative: Elliot Reyes is a 70-year-old woman in Dallas, Texas who is establishing care after a recent episode of urinary obstruction that resulted in placement of a right percutaneous nephrostomy tube. She lives alone in an apartment near her workplace and describes herself as driven and meticulous about schedules and health tasks, including tracking supplies for her nephrostomy care. She reports staying physically active with brisk treadmill walking about 5 days per week, and she tries to keep a structured routine despite intermittent right flank discomfort. Over the past several months, she has experienced fluctuating right-sided flank pain and variable nephrostomy drainage, prompting repeated primary care visits. At her new-patient intake, she described dull intermittent pain, occasional skin-site burning, and worry about whether the tube is draining normally. She has not reported persistent fever or severe systemic symptoms during office visits. Basic lab testing was obtained as part of procedural preparation, showing stable blood counts and mildly reduced kidney function. A renal ultrasound performed recently demonstrated mild-to-moderate right hydronephrosis with the nephrostomy catheter partially visualized, supporting ongoing concern for collecting system dilation. Her current working diagnoses include hydronephrosis with renal and ureteral calculous obstruction (N13.2), encounter for attention to an artificial urinary tract opening (Z43.6), and intermittent abdominal/flank pain (R10.9). She also has essential hypertension (I10) and morbid obesity due to excess calories (E66.01), which are relevant when coordinating imaging-guided procedures and monitoring vital signs. Family history is notable for kidney stones in her father and hypertension in her mother, and she reports that her adult sibling has had recurrent urinary tract infections. The current referral is for an injection procedure for nephrostogram and/or ureterogram (CPT 50394), requested to define collecting system and ureteral patency through the existing nephrostomy tube and to inform next-step tube management planning. She has used symptom-directed measures such as acetaminophen and hydration counseling but has limited documented longitudinal treatment detail available from prior care. She is engaged in counseling for stress related to managing a new medical device and balancing work demands, and she aims to proceed with the planned diagnostic evaluation to clarify anatomy before decisions about catheter change or removal are made.

pa_request.clinical_justification: Mateo Sosa has end-stage renal disease (N18.6) with ongoing dependence on hemodialysis (Z99.2) due to hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.0). After transplant committee approval and completed preoperative workup, renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360) is planned to transition from chronic dialysis dependence to transplant-based renal replacement therapy while coordinating management of anemia of CKD (D63.1) and recurrent hyperkalemia (E87.5).

pa_request.previous_treatments: Maintenance hemodialysis three times weekly with AV fistula access; management of ESRD-related complications with potassium binders (current Lokelma; prior patiromer discontinued due to constipation), phosphate binders (sevelamer), calcitriol for secondary hyperparathyroidism, and epoetin alfa for CKD anemia; antihypertensive therapy with amlodipine and carvedilol; prior lisinopril stopped due to hyperkalemia risk.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Transition from chronic hemodialysis dependence to transplant-based renal replacement therapy with improved overall metabolic stability and reduced dialysis-related symptom burden, with careful perioperative management of hypertension and CKD-related anemia.

115.bio_narrative: Mateo Sosa is a 63-year-old Hispanic male living in Dallas, Texas, who has progressed to end-stage renal disease (N18.6) and has been dependent on in-center hemodialysis (Z99.2) since 2024. His kidney disease is closely linked to long-standing hypertension and is documented as hypertensive chronic kidney disease with ESRD (I12.0). Over the last year, Mateo has worked to stabilize dialysis sessions and manage renal complications with the support of UT Southwestern Medical Center and his dialysis unit. He uses a pill organizer, keeps a consistent dialysis schedule, and tracks diet choices aimed at reducing potassium and phosphorus burden. In the months leading up to the current request, Mateo reported that dialysis is increasingly wearing him down, with fatigue and reduced stamina that interfere with maintaining his work routine and daily activities. At the specialist consult on 02-20-2026, he expressed a desire to move forward with kidney transplantation. Subsequent transplant surgery counseling on 03-25-2026 focused on the operative plan and the perioperative course, including discussion of recipient nephrectomy as part of the surgical strategy for transplantation (CPT 50360). Medical management has remained active and consistent with ESRD complications: recurrent hyperkalemia (E87.5) is addressed with sodium zirconium cyclosilicate on non-dialysis days and dialysis prescription adjustments; anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1) is treated with epoetin alfa during dialysis; and secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin (N25.81) is treated with sevelamer and calcitriol. Mateo’s preoperative workup includes objective testing intended to support safe transplant planning. A pre-transplant CBC/CMP panel on 04-02-2026 documented CKD-related anemia and potassium tendency consistent with his established diagnoses. CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast on 04-09-2026 provided anatomic detail of atrophic native kidneys and aortoiliac calcification patterns relevant to operative planning. On 04-03-2026, he was advised that cardiac clearance was required; this prompted cardiology evaluation and testing to assess cardiac structure and ischemia risk in the setting of ESRD and hypertension. He attended a pre-op evaluation on 04-10-2026 for medication instructions and perioperative coordination. Socially, Mateo is married and reports reliable support from his spouse for transportation and postoperative caregiving. He denies tobacco and illicit drug use and drinks alcohol socially a few times per month. He reports mild situational anxiety related to the transplant process and finances and remains engaged in monthly psychiatric medication management. Family history includes hypertension and kidney disease in first-degree relatives, reinforcing his long-term risk profile. With transplant committee approval and multidisciplinary workup underway, the requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy is the next step in his renal replacement therapy pathway.

115.behavioral_notes: Generally adherent with dialysis schedule and medications, using a pill organizer and reminders; occasionally becomes rigid about routines and gets irritable when appointment times change. Diet adherence is improving with low potassium and low phosphorus choices, though he admits occasional lapses when stressed at work. No current tobacco use; drinks alcohol socially a few times per month. Denies illicit drug use. Reports mild situational anxiety related to the transplant process and finances, engaged in medication management visits and uses walking and short home workouts on non-dialysis days when energy permits.

encounters.chief_complaint: Dialysis is wearing me down and I want to move forward with transplant.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 63-year-old male with ESRD (N18.6) on hemodialysis (Z99.2) presents for transplant nephrology consult. He reports increasing fatigue after dialysis, difficulty maintaining work schedule, and desire to pursue transplant. He reports adherence to dialysis sessions and medications; notes occasional dietary lapses. Denies current chest pain, fevers, or access infection symptoms.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and decreased exercise tolerance. Negative for chest pain, resting dyspnea, fevers/chills, and GI bleeding.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing, no acute distress. CV: RRR, no murmurs. Lungs: clear. Extremities: no edema. Left arm AV fistula with palpable thrill.

Assessment: ESRD on hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) due to hypertensive CKD (I12.0) with associated anemia of CKD (D63.1), secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin (N25.81), and hyperkalemia tendency (E87.5). Candidate for transplant evaluation.

Plan: Proceed with transplant evaluation pathway including imaging and lab work. Continue dialysis schedule and current meds including Lokelma, Renvela, calcitriol, and epoetin protocol. Reinforce low potassium/phosphorus diet. Coordinate with transplant surgery for consultation.
observations: Reports dialysis-associated fatigue and decreased stamina.
No evidence of access infection on exam.
Blood pressure elevated pre-dialysis day; on antihypertensives.

HPI: Patient with ESRD (N18.6) on hemodialysis (Z99.2) presents for transplant surgery consult. He seeks details on kidney transplant surgery and has questions about native kidney management. He notes intermittent flank discomfort and a sense of fullness, particularly when constipated or fluid overloaded. No fevers or urinary symptoms.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and intermittent flank discomfort. Negative for chest pain, fever, and active infection symptoms.

Physical Exam: Alert, cooperative. Abdomen soft, non-tender without peritoneal signs. Left arm AV fistula intact. No edema.

Assessment: ESRD on dialysis due to hypertensive CKD (I12.0). Comorbid CKD anemia (D63.1), secondary hyperparathyroidism (N25.81), and hyperkalemia history (E87.5). Surgical planning for renal transplant with consideration of recipient nephrectomy.

Plan: Continue transplant workup and obtain CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast for operative planning. Discussed risks including bleeding and need for transfusion given anemia, infection risk with immunosuppression, cardiovascular risk, and electrolyte management. Patient agrees with plan and will proceed with additional clearance steps.
observations: Understands transplant pathway; asks detailed questions about operative timeline.
Reports intermittent flank discomfort; imaging planned for anatomy assessment.
