encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 63-year-old male with ESRD (N18.6) on hemodialysis (Z99.2) presents for pre-op evaluation and medication reconciliation in preparation for renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360). He reports stable dialysis attendance and no recent infections. Mild anxiety regarding surgery timeline.

Review of Systems: Negative for fever, cough, chest pain. Positive for fatigue.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing. CV regular, lungs clear. No edema. AV fistula left arm with good thrill.

Assessment: ESRD on dialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) with hypertensive CKD (I12.0), anemia of CKD (D63.1), secondary hyperparathyroidism (N25.81), and history of hyperkalemia (E87.5). Pre-op readiness check.

Plan: Continue amlodipine and carvedilol; transplant anesthesia will provide final day-of-surgery instructions. Continue Lokelma on non-dialysis days; coordinate with nephrology for perioperative potassium plan. Continue Renvela with meals; hold morning dose on day of surgery (06-09-2026) and resume per transplant team. Continue calcitriol and epoetin per dialysis unit until surgery; coordinate last dialysis session timing (goal within 24 hours of surgery). Review allergies (penicillin and shellfish) for periop planning. Concurrent Care Reference: Patient is actively engaged in monthly Medication Management (Psychiatry) with Dr. Maya Lin, MD; transplant team aware for perioperative support. Patient advised to bring updated medication list and dialysis run sheets.
observations: Medication reconciliation completed; no anticoagulants reported.
Allergies reviewed for perioperative antibiotic planning.
Anxiety noted; connected to psychiatry medication management.

pa_request.clinical_justification: Patient has ESRD (N18.6) with dependence on renal dialysis (Z99.2) and ongoing uremic symptom burden and dialysis-related complications despite optimized medical management. Renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360) is planned to address definitive renal replacement needs and symptomatic native kidney disorder (N28.89) with recurrent UTIs (N39.0) in the setting of end-stage kidney failure.
supporting_diagnoses: I12.0 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 chronic kidney disease or end stage renal disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
N25.81 - Secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin
T82.868A - Other specified complication of vascular prosthetic devices, implants and grafts, initial encounter
N39.0 - Urinary tract infection, site not specified
N28.89 - Other specified disorders of kidney and ureter
pa_request.previous_treatments: Thrice-weekly in-center hemodialysis since 12-04-2023 (Z99.2), AV fistula creation and endovascular maintenance for access complications (T82.868A), antihypertensive therapy for I12.0/I10, ESA/iron therapy for D63.1, phosphate binder/vitamin D analog for N25.81, and prior antibiotic courses for N39.0.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Transition from chronic dialysis dependence with improved metabolic and volume stability compared with ongoing dialysis management; reduced dialysis access-related complication burden and decreased recurrent infection/pain burden related to symptomatic native kidneys.

114.bio_narrative: Caroline is a 64-year-old Houston resident who has lived with progressive kidney disease for several years, initially managed through primary care visits focused on controlling essential hypertension (I10) and slowing kidney decline. Over time, she developed complications typical of advanced kidney disease, including anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1) with persistent fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance, and mineral-bone abnormalities with secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin (N25.81) requiring ongoing binder and vitamin D analog therapy. Her kidney function eventually progressed to end stage renal disease (N18.6), and she began in-center hemodialysis on 12-04-2023. Since initiation of dialysis she has remained dependent on renal dialysis (Z99.2) three times per week. She describes the dialysis routine as physically draining, with predictable post-dialysis fatigue, intermittent nausea, pruritus, and fluctuating volume status that sometimes worsens dyspnea before treatments. She has a left radiocephalic AV fistula but has experienced periods of reduced access flow requiring evaluation and endovascular maintenance, consistent with a vascular access complication history (T82.868A). In addition to dialysis burden, she reports intermittent flank discomfort and has experienced recurrent outpatient-treated urinary tract infections over the past year (N39.0). Although her urine output is minimal, the pattern of symptoms and recurrent infections supports chronic symptomatic native kidney disorder (N28.89), which is relevant to the planned recipient nephrectomy component of the requested surgery. Socially, she lives alone and works intermittently in service and retail settings, balancing fatigue and medical appointments. She relies on a close friend for rides to select appointments and for support during major medical events. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol rarely, and denies illicit drug use. Family history is notable for hypertension and kidney disease, increasing her awareness of the long-term course. Given ongoing dialysis dependence (Z99.2), ESRD (N18.6) with hypertensive CKD/ESRD (I12.0), active CKD complications (D63.1, N25.81), access history (T82.868A), and recurrent UTIs with symptomatic native kidneys (N39.0, N28.89), she was referred for transplant evaluation. The current plan is renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360) scheduled for 06-02-2026 at Houston Methodist Hospital, following completion of nephrology, surgery, cardiology, and infectious disease evaluations.

114.behavioral_notes: Patient is generally adherent with dialysis attendance and prescribed medications, using a handwritten schedule and pill organizer; she becomes frustrated by regimen complexity but follows through when plans are clearly outlined. Diet adherence is variable; she attempts renal diet restrictions but reports occasional high-sodium convenience foods on workdays, contributing to interdialytic weight gain. She does not use tobacco, drinks alcohol rarely, and denies illicit drug use. She reports anxiety and irritability surrounding transplant uncertainty and finances; she is engaged in weekly CBT and uses walking and structured routines as coping strategies.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Evaluation of dysuria and suprapubic discomfort in a dialysis patient with prior UTI history; visit supports stabilization prior to transplant planning.
encounters.chief_complaint: Burning when I try to pee and lower belly pressure.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 64-year-old female with ESRD on hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) presents with 2 days of suprapubic pressure and dysuria with scant urine output. She reports similar episodes over the past year treated as UTIs. No flank pain escalation beyond baseline intermittent discomfort, and no fevers or chills. She is currently in transplant evaluation and wants symptoms addressed promptly.

Review of Systems: Positive for dysuria and suprapubic discomfort; negative for fever, rigors, vomiting, chest pain, or shortness of breath. Skin itching noted chronically after dialysis.

Physical Exam: Afebrile, non-toxic. Abdomen soft with mild suprapubic tenderness, no guarding. No CVA tenderness. AV fistula site clean with palpable thrill.

Assessment: Urinary tract infection symptoms (N39.0) in the setting of ESRD (N18.6) and dialysis dependence (Z99.2). Chronic symptomatic native kidney disorder (N28.89) noted with intermittent baseline flank discomfort.

Plan: Treated with cephalexin (completed course) and symptom monitoring instructions. Coordinate with nephrology/transplant team regarding infection documentation. Continue antihypertensive regimen and review home BP trends (I10).
observations: No CVA tenderness; baseline flank discomfort unchanged

images.findings: LVEF 55-60% with normal global systolic function; mild concentric LV hypertrophy and mild left atrial enlargement; mild diastolic dysfunction; no significant valvular stenosis and no pericardial effusion. Obtained as part of cardiac stratification in hypertensive CKD/ESRD (I12.0/I10) prior to requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360).

Bilateral atrophic native kidneys with cortical thinning consistent with ESRD (N18.6); no hydronephrosis and no perinephric abscess/obstruction. Aortoiliac calcifications without aneurysm. Study obtained for anatomic planning before requested CPT 50360 and to evaluate symptomatic native kidney disorder (N28.89) in the setting of recurrent UTI history (N39.0).

Patent left radiocephalic AV fistula with estimated flow ~720 mL/min and no evidence of acute thrombosis or high-grade stenosis. Study supports dialysis contingency planning (Z99.2) and documents access status in context of prior access complications (T82.868A) prior to requested CPT 50360.

reports.results: CBC: WBC 6.8 x10^3/µL, Hgb 9.4 g/dL, Plt 212 x10^3/µL. CMP: Na 137, K 5.1, CO2 21, BUN 58, Cr 9.8, Alb 3.6. Coags: INR 1.0. Mineral-bone: Phos 5.8, iPTH 620.
reports.notes: Findings document ESRD physiology (N18.6) on dialysis (Z99.2) with anemia in CKD (D63.1) and secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin (N25.81). Obtained within 2 weeks of the planned renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360) to support perioperative planning.

110.bio_narrative: Zoey Buffay is a 63-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas who has had a gradual decline in kidney function over several years in the setting of long-standing hypertension, now documented as end-stage renal disease (N18.6) with hypertensive chronic kidney disease, stage 5/ESRD (I12.0). Over the last 6–9 months she has reported increasing fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, and intermittent nausea, along with recurrent ankle and lower-leg swelling that tends to worsen later in the day. She has been trying to follow a low-sodium diet with variable success and has been avoiding NSAIDs; she uses acetaminophen for aches to reduce renal risk. Her symptom burden became more prominent in late 2025, leading to an ER visit on 11-18-2025 for inability to keep food down and worsening leg edema. In early 2026, she continued outpatient follow-up for persistent exhaustion and edema. Objective lab monitoring has shown advanced kidney failure with complications including metabolic acidosis (E87.2), treated with sodium bicarbonate, and anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1), supported with oral ferrous sulfate. Despite symptom-directed volume management with furosemide for localized edema (R60.0) and ongoing blood pressure control with amlodipine, she continues to experience fluctuating edema and low energy. In preparation for renal replacement therapy planning, she completed a renal lab trend panel (04-02-2026) and a lab update (04-14-2026), and she underwent vascular mapping ultrasound for dialysis access planning (CPT 93990) on 04-08-2026. A chest X-ray (CPT 71046) and ECG (CPT 93000) were obtained in early April as part of baseline cardiopulmonary assessment given her volume-related symptoms and to support safe planning for dialysis initiation. Zoey lives independently and relies on her sister (emergency contact) for transportation to complex medical appointments. She is a former light smoker, drinks alcohol rarely, and denies illicit drug use. She endorses situational anxiety around medical visits and the transition to dialysis and remains engaged in active psychotherapy every two weeks. Family history is notable for hypertension and kidney disease in first-degree relatives, which she reports has influenced her decision to pursue structured renal care planning. As of 04-16-2026, the chart reflects a dialysis plan with completed preliminary workup; care coordination is ongoing and she has expressed uncertainty about which nephrology clinician will be overseeing the dialysis prescription and follow-through.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient has end-stage renal disease with ongoing uremic symptoms, recurrent hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, and volume overload despite outpatient medical therapy. Dialysis is requested to manage persistent electrolyte/acid-base derangements and symptomatic uremia while coordinating anemia and mineral bone disease management in the setting of diabetes and hypertensive CKD.

Objective evidence: 04-11-2026: Potassium 2.0 mEq/L | 04-09-2026: Creatinine 8.7 mg/dL, BUN 92 mg/dL, CO2 16 mmol/L, Potassium 6.1 mmol/L, Hgb 8.7 g/dL.

221.bio_narrative: Din Djarin is a 72-year-old widowed man living in Houston, Texas, who previously worked in building security and now does occasional night-shift contract work. He is quiet, structured in routines, and tends to under-report symptoms until they begin to interfere with function. Over the last several years he has managed long-standing type 2 diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease (E11.22) and hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 chronic kidney disease/end stage renal disease (I12.0). Despite consistent primary care follow-up, his renal function steadily worsened, and by late 2025 he progressed to chronic kidney disease, stage 5 (N18.5) with increasing difficulty maintaining potassium and bicarbonate levels. In early 2026, he noted worsening fatigue (R53.83), reduced appetite, intermittent nausea (R11.0), and diffuse itching (L29.9). He also developed progressively worsening bilateral lower extremity swelling (R60.0) and occasional shortness of breath at night when fluid retention increased. Outpatient laboratory monitoring in early April 2026 showed findings consistent with end stage renal disease (N18.6) with complications including hyperkalemia (E87.5) and metabolic acidosis (E87.2), along with anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1) and secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin (N25.81). A renal ultrasound demonstrated small echogenic kidneys without obstruction, supporting chronic irreversible medical renal disease. He has tried conservative medical management including dietary sodium/potassium restriction, high-dose diuretic therapy for edema, sodium bicarbonate for acidosis, and potassium binder therapy for recurrent hyperkalemia. He previously underwent bridge vascular access management with a tunneled dialysis catheter and has a left upper extremity arteriovenous fistula created in December 2025, now with a continuous thrill/bruit on exam. His family history is notable for a father with type 2 diabetes and kidney failure requiring dialysis and a mother with longstanding hypertension and stroke. He lives alone but has reliable support from a close family friend who provides transportation when needed. Given persistent uremic symptoms and repeated metabolic complications despite outpatient therapy, nephrology is requesting initiation/continuation of outpatient maintenance dialysis (CPT 90999) at Houston Methodist Dialysis Center - Texas Medical Center with an expected start date of 05-20-2026. The care plan includes close monitoring of potassium and bicarbonate, coordinated anemia and mineral bone disease protocols, and ongoing blood pressure and diabetes medication adjustments during dialysis initiation.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient has end-stage renal disease with progressive decline in kidney function and persistent uremic symptoms despite optimized medical management. Recent laboratory evaluation demonstrates severely reduced renal function with metabolic acidosis and recurrent hyperkalemia episodes requiring repeated urgent management. Comorbid chronic diastolic heart failure and hypertensive kidney disease increase risk from ongoing electrolyte and volume instability, supporting initiation of outpatient hemodialysis.

Objective evidence: 03-29-2026: Creatinine 8.1 (high), BUN 92 (high), CO2 17 (low), Potassium 5.9 (high), Hgb 9.1 (low) | 03-29-2026: Hgb 9.1 g/dL.
supporting_diagnoses: I12.0 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 chronic kidney disease or end stage renal disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Renal diet counseling with potassium/sodium restriction, loop diuretic therapy for volume control, oral sodium bicarbonate therapy for metabolic acidosis, antihypertensive regimen adjustments, and anemia management with iron supplementation.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Stabilization of electrolytes and acid-base status and improved volume management with structured outpatient hemodialysis monitoring and ongoing nephrology follow-up.

111.bio_narrative: Cassian Korr is a 71-year-old man living in Houston, Texas, who has been followed for long-standing hypertension complicated by progressive chronic kidney disease. Over the last 18–24 months he noticed a slow but steady decline in stamina, with increasing fatigue and difficulty completing his usual daily tasks. In the past 2–3 months he developed more prominent symptoms consistent with advanced kidney failure, including reduced appetite, intermittent nausea, and persistent generalized itching. He also reports fluctuating ankle swelling and exertional shortness of breath that worsen with higher sodium intake, which is clinically important given his known chronic diastolic heart failure. He has engaged closely with outpatient care, attending primary care and nephrology visits and working to follow a renal diet. Despite these steps, recent lab monitoring has shown persistent complications of end-stage renal disease (N18.6), including recurrent hyperkalemia (E87.5) and metabolic acidosis (E87.21) despite oral bicarbonate therapy. He has chronic normocytic anemia consistent with anemia of chronic kidney disease (D63.1), contributing to low energy and reduced exercise tolerance. Blood pressure has remained challenging to control, consistent with hypertensive chronic kidney disease with ESRD (I12.0), and volume sensitivity related to heart failure has contributed to localized edema (R60.0) and episodic dyspnea (R06.02). Cassian previously worked in logistics and prefers a highly structured routine; he lives alone but has reliable support from his adult daughter, who assists with transportation and appointment scheduling. He is a former smoker and does not use illicit drugs. He has reported increased anxiety about his declining health and has engaged in brief medication management for adjustment disorder with anxiety (F43.22), which is being coordinated around the anticipated dialysis schedule. Family history is notable for hypertension and kidney disease in his father, and heart disease in his mother. Given the documented progression to ESRD with ongoing uremic symptoms and objective electrolyte/acid-base abnormalities despite optimized medical management, nephrology completed pre-dialysis education and initiated vascular access planning. The current referral is to begin outpatient dialysis at Houston Methodist Hospital with a planned start date of 05-20-2026 to provide controlled solute clearance and fluid management while continuing multidisciplinary monitoring.

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 71-year-old man with hypertensive chronic kidney disease and known advanced renal dysfunction presents with 6–8 weeks of worsening fatigue, lower appetite, and increasing ankle swelling. He reports occasional shortness of breath when climbing stairs and notes his home blood pressure readings have been higher despite taking medications as prescribed. Denies chest pain or syncope.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue, pruritus at times, ankle edema, and exertional dyspnea. Negative for fever, productive cough, hematemesis, or focal neurologic deficits.

Physical Exam: Appears tired but not in acute distress. Cardiovascular: regular rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs: clear to auscultation, no rales. Extremities: 1+ bilateral ankle edema. Abdomen: soft, non-tender.

Assessment: Concern for progression to ESRD (N18.6) with volume sensitivity and anemia; hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.0) with suboptimal BP control; edema (R60.0) and dyspnea (R06.02) in setting of chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32).

Plan: Increase loop diuretic dosing for edema control; reinforce low sodium diet and daily weights. Order BMP and CBC to evaluate electrolytes, bicarbonate, and anemia. Refer to nephrology urgently given symptomatic progression and suspected ESRD. Discussed red flags including worsening shortness of breath, palpitations, severe weakness, or confusion.
